coredata.dc:title: Natural biomass carbon Dots-Based fluorescence sensor for high precision detection of vitamin B12 in serum
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jueling
Mao, Chunling
Ye, Heng
Gao, Xun
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: Vitamin B12(Vit B12) is an essential micronutrient for body growth, and abnormal levels of Vit B12 in the human body are closely associated with the prediction of certain diseases. Hence, a rapid, sensitive, and environment-friendly approach for Vit B12 detection was set up. Herein, the Bird's nest carbon dots (B-CDs) are synthesized by using a bird's nest and distilled water as precursors. One-step hydrothermal synthesis has created B-CDs without toxic ingredients or surface chemical modifications. The prepared B-CDs exhibited outstanding characteristics including excellent water solubility, brilliant fluorescence performance great biocompatibility, and fine stability in a broad pH range of 3.0–11.0 and high ionic strength solution. The experiment revealed that the fluorescence of the reaction system showed a regular decrease after the interaction of B-CDs with Vit B12. Additionally, there was an excellent linear relationship between the F/F0 of B-CDs and the concentration of Vit B12. The linear range was 0 ∼ 100 µM, R 2 was 0.9929, and the detection limit was 0.24 µM. Finally, the proposed method successfully detected Vit B12 in human serum samples with recoveries of 96.2 %-100.3 %, showing broad clinical prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Fluorescence quenching
Carbon dots
VitaminB12 detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of B-CDs by Vit B12
Selectivity of B-CDs for Vit B12
Fluorescence detection of Vit B12 in serum
Explore the quenching mechanism of Vit B12

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the synthesis and mechanism of B-CDs.
Transmission electron micrographs and particle size distribution of B-CDs.
(a) The XPS characteristics of B-CDs. (b) The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s. (c) The high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s. (d) The high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s.
(a) UV–vis spectra of B-CDs (Insert in (a) was the image of B-CDs under fluorescence)of B-CDs）. (b) Emission spectra of B-CDs for varying excitation wavelengths; (c) FT-IR spectra of B-CDs.
The fluorescence stability of B-CDs in different（a）pH；（b）ionic strength；（c）Xe lamp irradiation and（d）the fluorescence intensity was measured by B-CDs continuously for ten days.

(a) The impact of varying ions on the fluorescence of B-CDs. (b) The impact of varying amino acids on the fluorescence of B-CDs. (c) The impact of macrocyclic structure substances and vitamin B group on the fluorescence of B-CDs.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs in the presence of varying concentrations of Vit B12. (b) The linear relationship between different concentrations of Vit B12 and B-CDs (n = 3).
(a)Fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs. (b) Zeta potential of B-CDs, Vit B12, B-CDs/ Vit B12.

(a) The effect of varying concentrations of B-CDs on the survival rate of the three cell types was assessed 24 h after exposure. After treatment with B-CDs, cell morphological images at 0 μg-mL-1 and 50 μg-mL-1. (b), (c) for MEF; (d) and (e) for HCT-116; (f) and (g) for Hepal 1–6.
Detection of Vit B12 in Human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Natural biomass carbon Dots-Based fluorescence sensor for high precision detection of vitamin B12 in serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning, 110016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Co-Innovation Center of Jiangsu Marine Bio-Industry Technology, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang222001,China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Co-Innovation Center of Jiangsu Marine Bio-Industry Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Co-Innovation Center of Jiangsu Marine Bio-Industry Technology, Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang222001,China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Jiangsu Ocean University, Lianyungang, Jiangsu, 222001, China.

ce:note-para.ce:italic: The corresponding authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Vitamin B12(Vit B12) is an essential micronutrient for body growth, and abnormal levels of Vit B12 in the human body are closely associated with the prediction of certain diseases. Hence, a rapid, sensitive, and environment-friendly approach for Vit B12 detection was set up. Herein, the Bird's nest carbon dots (B-CDs) are synthesized by using a bird's nest and distilled water as precursors. One-step hydrothermal synthesis has created B-CDs without toxic ingredients or surface chemical modifications. The prepared B-CDs exhibited outstanding characteristics including excellent water solubility, brilliant fluorescence performance great biocompatibility, and fine stability in a broad pH range of 3.0–11.0 and high ionic strength solution. The experiment revealed that the fluorescence of the reaction system showed a regular decrease after the interaction of B-CDs with Vit B12. Additionally, there was an excellent linear relationship between the F/F0 of B-CDs and the concentration of Vit B12. The linear range was 0 ∼ 100 µM, R 2 was 0.9929, and the detection limit was 0.24 µM. Finally, the proposed method successfully detected Vit B12 in human serum samples with recoveries of 96.2 %-100.3 %, showing broad clinical prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid and easy detection of a wide range of potentially hazardous analytes is essential for analytical and materials chemistry academics.[16] Vit B12 is a necessary nutrient that is obtained from animal products. The levels of Vit B12 in Serum are an effective monitoring indicator in the scientific analysis of some ailments including Vit B12 deficiency [17], autoimmune thyroid diseases [18], nervous system syndrome [19], and pernicious anemia [20]. At the same time, meta-analyses have demonstrated that Vit B12 deficiency is related to an improved threat of gestational diabetes mellitus in pregnant, so levels of Vit B12 are closely linked to human well-being. [21] In the clinical aspect, the levels of Vit B12 in Serum are helpful for diagnosing clinical diseases and determining nutritional status [22], even constructing a disease risk prediction model based on the level of Vit B12 in Serum [23].

At present, the quantitative method for the determination of Vit B12 includes high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [24], the electrochemical sensor method [25], microbiological assay [26], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [27], and fluorescence analysis. Microbiological assay methods are known as a gold standard, with high sensitivity and low detection limit. Due to cumbersome operations and long experiment periods, the microbiological method is unfit for testing large quantities of samples. Meanwhile, other instrumental analysis methods mentioned above are frequently employed to determine samples with high Vit B12 content, such as medicines and health care products, typically at the mg/g level. [28] The levels of Vit B12 in food and serum are usually very low, requiring high sensitivity with low detection limits. [29].

This indicates the potential value of developing a convenient, rapid, and stable technique for the quantification of Vit B12 for clinical applications. Using B-CDs as a fluorescence probe in this research enables the accurate detection of Vit B12 with heightened sensitivity low detection limit, cost-effectiveness, easy experimental operation, and other determination techniques that can complement each other, which provide new research ideas for clinical examination.

Bird's Nest (Yangzijiang Pharmaceutical Group Co., LTD.). Human serum samples (The Ethics Committee has granted authorization for the use of human serum samples obtained with the assistance of the First Affiliated Hospital of Jilin University). Distilled water, potable water (Wahaha Group Co., LTD.), sodium chloride (NaCl, analytical pure), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, analytical pure) bought from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCL, analytical pure) was once bought from RionLon (Tianjin) Chemical Co., LTD. (Tianjin, China). Standard vitamin B12 (98 % purity), valine (Val, analytical pure), leucine (Leu, analytical pure), methionine (Met, analytical pure), serine (Ser, analytical pure), threonine (Thr, analytical pure), arginine (Arg, analytical pure), vitamin B1 (analytical pure), vitamin B6 (analytical pure), folic acid（97 % purity）, vitaminC (analytical pure), chlorophyll(analytical pure), protohemin(95 % purity), got from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China).

Zhongjia LC-400 Low-speed centrifuge (Anhui Zhongke Zhongjia Scientific Instrument Co., LTD.), HN202 Thermostatic electric drying oven (Nantong Hunan Scientific Instrument Co., LTD.), KQ5200 ultrasonic cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., LTD.). Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy used to be acquired with the usage of a TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd. China). The excitation and emission spectra had been measured via an F97 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Language Technology Co., Ltd., China). TEM pix had been taken by way of a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) used to be carried out with the usage of Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700 FT-IR (Thermo Fisher Co., Waltham, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) had been recorded with the aid of a Thermo Fisher EscaLab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Co., Waltham, USA). The cytotoxicity of B-CDs was recorded using a Mode Varioskan LUX microplate reader (Singapore). The confocal fluorescence pictures of B-CDs labeled cells have been obtained with EVOS-FL-Auto-2 inverted fluorescence microscope (Invitrogen, USA) below ambient conditions.

Bird's nests are a natural carbon source that can be produced as B-CDs by a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. Briefly, 5.43 g edible bird's nest was added to purified water with ultrasound for 5 min. Afterward, the blend was transferred to an autoclave with a Teflon lining of 20 mL capacity and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The product was purified in a centrifuge for 10 min to remove reaction slag and particulate matter. The solution was then filtered through a microporous filter membrane to obtain a yellowish-brown stock solution of B-CDs. This solution was used for characterization and application purposes.

To evaluate the toxicity of B-CDs, the CCK-8 colorimetric assay to assess their cytotoxicity and biocompatibility. [30–32] In this experiment, the MEF (mouse embryonic fibroblast progenitor cells), Hepal-6 (mouse liver cancer cells), and HCT-116 (human colon cancer cells) were selected as model cells to determine their cell survival rates under the conditions of adding B-CDs. Initially, individual wells of a 96-well plate were filled with a range of 5,000 to 10,000 cells, which were then cultivated for 24 h before being exposed to different concentrations of B-CDs solution (100 μL) in each well. For each of the five concentrations (0 µg/mL, 15 µg/mL, 30 µg/mL, 60 µg/mL, 120 µg/mL), three samples were run in parallel. At the end of the incubation, a 10 μL CCK-8 solution was added to each well and placed in the incubator. The mixture was incubated in an incubator at 37 °C for 4 h, after which the medium was gently aspirated from each well, formaldehyde dissolved, and then agitated at 85 rpm for 15 min on a shaker at a constant temperature. Finally, the cell survival rate can be calculated in accordance with the following equation: cell viability (%) = [(As-Ab)/(Ac-Ab)] × 100 %. (As = absorbance of the examined sample, Ab = absorbance of clean sample, Ac = absorbance of manipulated group sample).[33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of B-CDs by Vit B12
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a sequence of Vit B12 standard solutions (0.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, 30.0, 35.0, 40.0, 45.0, 50.0, 55.0, 60.0, 65.0, 70.0, 75.0, 80.0, 90.0, 100.0 μmol/L) was prepared. Then, 150 μL B-CDs stock solution was added to 1 mL of Vit B12 standard solution. Afterward, the solution was thinned down to a volume of 3 mL, and shaken for 8 min. Finally, the fluorescence patterns of the mixture were measured using the fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 386 nm in the surrounding environment.

The selectivity of B-CDs is an essential parameter for practical applications, which can determine whether B-CDs have good selectivity and resistance to interference in humans by evaluating the effect of common ions and amino acids on fluorescence intensity. Various ions (Ca2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Li+, NH4+, HCO3–, Ni2+, Co2+) and common amino acids (Ser, Leu, His, Val, Thr, Met, Gln) were chosen for the purpose of exploring B-CDs selectivity, with a concentration of 5 mM. The fluorescence excitation wavelength of 386 nm was used to record all measurements under regular conditions. In order to better prove the anti-interference of B-CDs, the Vit B group, and macrocyclic structural substances were added to the mixture, including vitamin B6, vitamin B1, folic acid, vitamin C, chlorophyll, and protohemin, with a concentration of 10 µM.

In order to understand the luminescence mechanism of B-CDs, it is crucial to have knowledge of their surface structure as it is responsible for the compounding of electrons and holes that result in fluorescence emission. The transmission electron microscopy scans of B-CDs in Fig. 2 (a) show that the B-CDs particles obtained are monodisperse and homogeneous spheres, and the mean grain size of B-CDs is about 0.76 um in Fig. 2(b). Fig. 3 provides information regarding the elemental makeup and chemical bonding of B-CDs through XPS analysis. Based on the full XPS spectrum, the three peaks about C1s(284.77 eV), N1s(399.31 eV), and O1s (531.46 eV) indicate that the surface of B-CDs contains the above three elements at 72.03 %, 12.14 %, and 15.83 % respectively. Further analysis of C–C (284.7 eV), C-N (285 eV), and C = O (287.7 eV) as the primary forms of carbon present. [34,35] The pyridine nitrogen C-N-C (399.9 eV), C = N (398.4 eV), and N–H (401.29 eV) are the primary forms of nitrogen present. [36] The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1S can be fitted to three peaks at 531.34 eV, 532.45 eV, and 530.6 eV, corresponding to the –OH, -C-O, and HO-C = O groups, respectively. [37,38] The presence of numerous oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the surface of B-CDs promotes their ability to dissolve in water and remain evenly dispersed in aqueous solutions.

The optical properties of B-CDs were characterized by fluorescence spectrophotometry, UV–Vis, and FT-IR. It can be viewed in Fig. 4 (a) that B-CDs exhibit an obvious blue luminescence pattern by the UV lamp (λ = 365 nm). Meanwhile, Fig. 4(a) shows the UV–Vis spectra of the B-CDs and the reaction system (B-CDs + Vit B12). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum scan shows that B-CDs and the reaction system has a distinct UV absorption peak at 283 nm, caused by the sp2 hybridized carbon of B-CDs π- π* leap. [39] After analyzing the spectrum presented in Fig. 4(b), we have concluded that the intensity and peak position of the fluorescence emission is directly influenced by the excitation wavelength, prompting us to investigate the fluorescence properties of B-CDs at specific excitation wavelengths. The emission peak is red-shifted when the excitation wavelength gradually increases from 320 nm to 500 nm. It was determined that the most effective emission wavelength for B-CDs was 386 nm, with a maximum emission wavelength of 471 nm. As a result, setting the excitation wavelength to 386 nm is the most favorable choice for detecting Vit B12 under ideal spectral conditions. Fig. 4 (c) presents the results of the FT-IR analysis used to determine the surface functional groups and structures of the B-CDs. The strong and wide absorption band observed at 3401 cm-1can be attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H/O–H. [40,41] The characteristic peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 1666 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibrations of the C-N and C = O bonds. [41] The C-O stretching vibrations are responsible for the peak observed at 1072 cm−1. [42,43] The above functional groups make the surface of B-CDs easily modifiable and increase their hydrophilicity in aqueous solutions.

Exploring the steadfastness of B-CDs is essential to provide reliable reference experimental data for the use of B-CDs in the process. Fig. 5 (a) shows the variation in fluorescence strength of B-CDs under different acidic and alkaline environments. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs shows a relatively stable state in acidic and alkaline environments with pH values of 3–11. In pH 1–2 and pH 12–13 environments, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs was greatly affected, especially in alkaline environments, where the decrease in fluorescence intensity was more obvious. [44] The instability of the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs in extremely acidic and alkaline environments is due to the binding of carboxylates to protons increasing the ratio of non-radiative to radiative decay of CDs through excited state proton transfer, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. [45] Fig. 5(b) shows the influence of various salt concentrations on the fluorescence strength of B-CDs. When the salt concentration was increased to 2.0 mol/L, the fluorescence strength was almost unchanged and stable. Fig. 5(c) surveyed the photostability of bird's nest B-CDs. The fluorescence strength of B-CDs was measured after sequential light exposure with an Xe lamp for 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 min. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs was relatively stable over 30 min which showed it had good photostability. According to Fig. 5(d), the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs was measured continuously over a period of ten days. Furthermore, the B-CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 10 d and tested daily. The fluorescence intensity value of the B-CDs remained above 98 % of the initial value throughout this period. It showed that the best-use period of the prepared B-CDs is 10 d, which may be related to the biological matrix itself, such as the bird's nest. The above results showed that B-CDs had a certain storage stability and reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of B-CDs for Vit B12
ce:section.ce:para: To better explore the application of B-CDs as a fluorescence probe in the actual process, the response of B-CDs to 12 kinds of ions, including Ca2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Li+, NH4+, HCO3–, Ni2+ and Co2+ was investigated. In Fig. 6 (a), it can be seen that only Vit B12 can significantly induce fluorescence quenching reaction with B-CDs. Meanwhile, the response degree of B-CDs and 7 common amino acids, including Ser, Leu, His, Val, Thr, Met, and Gln, was also investigated to further explore the selectivity of B-CDs for Vit B12. The result is shown in Fig. 6(b), where only Vit B12 can be used as a fluorescence quencher for B-CDs. To demonstrate the selectivity of B-CDs, we assayed substances from the Vitamin B group and macrocyclic structures. Fig. 6(c)illustrates that B-CDs exhibited the most significant fluorescence quenching for Vit B12.In summary, B-CDs have promising selectivity for Vit B12 and good resistance to impurities that may be encountered.

In order to determine the optimal experimental conditions for the entire reaction system of B-CDs with Vit B12., the conditions of pH, reaction time, the volume of Vit B12. added, and the dilution ratio of human serum were optimized. The experimental results demonstrated that the reaction system exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity at pH = 7, a reaction time of 5 min, the addition of 1.00 mL of Vit B12, and a dilution ratio of 100 for human serum. Furthermore, the order of addition of reagents did not have any impact.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Vit B12 in serum
ce:section.ce:para: At a wavelength of 386 nm, the F/F0 of B-CDs exhibited a regular decline with an increase in Vit B12 concentrations, as illustrated in Fig. 7 (a) The fluorescence intensity ratio of the reaction system exhibited a noticeable linear correlation with the concentration of Vit B12 aqueous solution between 0 and 100 µM, as demonstrated in Fig. 7 (b). After further linear regression, the linear fitting equation was obtained as F/F0 = -0.002C (Vit B12) + 1.011, with a correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.9929. By employing the prescribed methodology from the literature, the detection limit is 0.24 µM. It was established for the LOD (3σ/k), wherein σ denotes the fluorescent response's standard deviation and k represents the line's slope. To enhance the practical examination of B-CDs as fluorescent probes, the actual sample for determination was obtained by diluting serum by a factor of 100. The results are shown in Table 1 . The spiked recoveries for Vit B12 were in the range of 96.2 % −100.3 %. The results of this method demonstrate that the prepared B-CDs have a high recovery of Vit B12 in serum, which proves that the method has excellent applicability in clinical assays. Undoubtedly, compared to other references (Table 2 ), using B-CDs as fluorescent sensors can be sensitive and specific for detecting Vit B12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Explore the quenching mechanism of Vit B12
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching type of the system can be judged by the change in the UV spectrum and the determination of fluorescence lifetime. According to the UV spectrum of the system, there was a clear absorption peak of B-CDs at 283 nm. The addition of Vit B12 did not change the maximum absorption wavelength, suggesting that a new ground state complex was not produced.[46] It was speculated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was not a static quenching. The weighted average fluorescence lifetime was utilized to determine the change in the fluorescence lifetime of the system before and after the introduction of Vit B12. The results showed a decrease in fluorescence lifetime from 5.32 to 4.93 ns following the addition of Vit B12. Fig. 8 shows that the fluorescence lifetime decreased, and it can be judged that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is dynamic. [47] According to the literature, the internal filtration effect requires two conditions: no new peaks in the UV spectrum and the average fluorescence lifetime remains unchanged so the internal filtration effect can be excluded. [48,49] In addition, the structural characteristics of Vit B12 were analyzed and it can be inferred that the fluorescence quenching is due to electron transfer between B-CDs and Vit B12. Vit B12 has an imidazole structure, and the lone pair of electrons of the two N atoms on the imidazole ring can easily increase the electron cloud density on the C atom and enhance the reactivity, which can easily interact with B-CDs in the excited state, so it has excellent electron transferability. It can be speculated that Vit B12 may interact with excited B-CDs as an electron acceptor, causing dynamic charge transfer to the B-CDs, resulting in a fluorescence quenching. To verify this speculation, zeta potential tests have been done. As can be seen in Fig. 9 , with the addition of Vit B12, the zeta potential of B-CDs and Vit B12 decreased (from 10.2, 16.1 to 4.33 mV). The stability of the system increases as the absolute value of the zeta potential increases. Conversely, a smaller absolute value of the zeta potential indicates a greater tendency for the system to be disrupted and aggregated. The positive and negative zeta potential values correspond to the positive and negative surface charges of the system. Both B-CDs and Vit B12 have positive surface charges. Upon the addition of Vit B12, electron transfer occurs between B-CDs and Vit B12. The positive and negative charges on the system's surface are attracted to each other, leading to the condensation of the system and a significant decrease in the zeta potential value. In summary, the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs and Vit B12 was realized on the basis of electron transfer.

To evaluate the safety and biocompatibility of B-CDs, the cytotoxicity, and biocompatibility were assessed by determining the cell viability of three model cells. On the basis of the above experimental approach, the effects of different concentrations (15–120 μg/ml) of B-CDs solution on cell survival were observed. After a 24 h incubation period, the cell survival rate remained above 95 %, even when exposed to the highest concentration of B-CDs. As shown in Fig. 9(a) MEF cell survival was 95.2 %, Hepal-6 cell survival was 98.6 % and HCT-116 cell survival was 98.5 %, with all three cell survival rates above 95 %. Fig. 9 shows that fluorescence inverted microscopy images of the three cultured model cells, as shown in Fig. 9(b), where the MEF cell control was elongated and the morphology of the cultured cells did not change after the addition of 50 μg/mL B-CDs and remained intact. In Fig. 9(d), the control HCT-116 cells were grown on the wall adductively and no significant change in cell morphology was observed after 24 h of culture. In Fig. 9(f), the number and morphology of Hepal-6 cells did not change significantly after 24 h of culture, and the cell morphology was good. The results indicated that the B-CDs synthesized by bird's nest as the carbon material were low in toxicity and had good cytocompatibility.

This study introduces a novel one-step fluorescent probe created using a combination of a natural bird's nest and distilled water. The probe is cost-effective, environmentally friendly, and exhibits a green color. It can be utilized for the quantitative determination of Vit B12 in serum. B-CDs have excellent stability, aqueous solution dispersion, good fluorescence response, and strong anti-interference properties. The use of natural biomass as a carbon resource in the preparation of B-CDs was found to result in low cytotoxicity based on the results obtained from both the CCK-8 method and microscopic observation. A linear relationship was established after determining the optimal experimental conditions by using the quenching effect of Vit B12 on B-CDs. This work indicates that the utilization of B-CDs proves to be a reliable and efficient approach to detecting Vit B12, presenting a novel technique and concept for clinical analysis due to its extensive range and low detection limit.

Jueling Chen: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Chunling Mao: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Heng Ye: . Xun Gao: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Resources. Longshan Zhao: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project No. : 82104349), Jiangsu Provincial Key Discipline Construction Project (PAPD).
